---
title: "Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint"
chapter_id: "context-transactions-snapshots-mounts-and-taint"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "vcm_public"
  - "ladon_manhattan"
  - "context_engineer"
  - "black_hole_context_manager"
  - "vcm_editable"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `context-transactions-snapshots-mounts-and-taint` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `vcm_public`, `ladon_manhattan`, `context_engineer`, `black_hole_context_manager`, `vcm_editable` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `vcm_public`; supporting: `ladon_manhattan`, `context_engineer`, `black_hole_context_manager`; connector/recovery: `vcm_editable` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `vcm_public`, `vcm_editable`; raw cache: `vcm_public`, `ladon_manhattan`, `context_engineer`, `black_hole_context_manager`; pending source notes: `ladon_manhattan`, `context_engineer`, `black_hole_context_manager`; connector/recovery: `vcm_editable` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter is a v0.2 living-book draft. It synthesizes the architecture manifest, the current outline, the source inventory, available source notes, and local raw-source readiness. It does not by itself promote any claim beyond the support state shown in the chapter metadata and Appendix C. Where the chapter uses design reasoning, it is written as architecture argument rather than as reported empirical fact.

## Problem

Memory and context need runtime semantics for consistency, branches, contradiction, deletion, privacy, and taint.

Within the ASI Stack, this is an operational cognition layer problem. The layer receives governed goals, command contracts, memory packets, beliefs, work orders, and tool requests and must turn them into typed plans, context selections, claims, jobs, artifacts, approvals, and replayable execution records. The hard part is not naming the capability; it is making the boundary explicit enough that a later layer can test, replay, reject, or replace it without guessing what authority was assumed.

It inherits pressure from the previous chapter, Semantic Pages, Context Cells, and Certificates, and makes that pressure operational in a narrower boundary. It hands its output to Verification Bandwidth and Context Adequacy, where the next layer tightens or implements the same constraint. That is why the chapter is written as part of a single stack rather than as a standalone paper summary.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A retrieval store cannot guarantee read-your-writes behavior, copy-on-write experimentation, deletion closure, or taint propagation by itself.

In this chapter, the failure is more specific than bad prompting or weak model choice: a retrieval store cannot guarantee read-your-writes behavior, copy-on-write experimentation, deletion closure, or taint propagation by itself. If `context-transactions-snapshots-mounts-and-taint` is left implicit, downstream layers inherit a capability whose authority, evidence burden, and rollback conditions are unclear. The stack therefore treats the missing contract as the defect, not merely the local model behavior.

This does not mean that every layer must be heavyweight. The stack is efficient precisely because small, typed artifacts can carry constraints forward while large models, tools, humans, or specialist modules are invoked only where the current boundary demands them.

## Core Claim

[context-transactions-snapshots-mounts-and-taint.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] VCM should use transactional memory semantics: immutable events, versioned pages, snapshots, mounts, taint, temporal validity, and deletion closure.

The claim is architectural: it says how this layer should be shaped if the whole system is expected to remain governed under growth. The current support state is `argument`, so the claim should be read as a design rationale unless a later evidence bundle promotes it. The assigned source set - Virtual_Context_Memory_v1, Ladon & The Manhattan Protocol, Context Engineer / Manhattan Protocol, Black Hole Context Manager, and Virtual_Context_Memory_v1.0_Editable - gives the chapter its vocabulary and comparison points, but the public manuscript keeps source-derived support separate from the draft itself.

## Mechanism

The mechanism turns the core claim into a working contract. First, record immutable memory events. Second, compile task snapshots with mounts and copy-on-write branches. Third, propagate taint and deletion obligations through derived context.

- Record immutable memory events.
- Compile task snapshots with mounts and copy-on-write branches.
- Propagate taint and deletion obligations through derived context.

Operationally, the layer should produce artifacts that can be inspected without rerunning the whole cognition path. A downstream reader or executor should be able to locate the relevant inputs, constraints, residuals, authority grants, and failure conditions. That artifact discipline is what lets the stack improve without making the improvement path opaque.

For implementation, the mechanism should be kept small at first: a schema, a ledger entry, a route decision, a context packet, a proof obligation, or a replayable job record. Only after that minimal form is testable should the layer absorb richer heuristics, learned routing, larger memory, or autonomous repair.

## Interfaces

The interface for Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint is the contract that lets adjacent layers compose without absorbing each other's authority. It should make the chapter's output legible to planning, memory, reasoning, execution, evidence, and improvement layers while preserving the boundary described by the core claim.

- Planning receives consistent views.
- Security labels sensitive mounts.
- Evidence records contradictions and supersession.

The upstream interface accepts the part-level inputs: governed goals, command contracts, memory packets, beliefs, work orders, and tool requests. The downstream interface emits typed plans, context selections, claims, jobs, artifacts, approvals, and replayable execution records. When either side is ambiguous, the layer should fail closed by producing a missing-contract, missing-evidence, or missing-authority record instead of silently continuing.

This interface also makes chapter insertion and reordering safer. A new chapter can be added when it introduces a genuinely new contract, invariant, or artifact type; otherwise it should merge into the layer that already owns the boundary.

## Invariants

The invariants are the conditions that must remain true even when the implementation changes, the source set grows, or later chapters introduce stronger mechanisms.

- Snapshots are consistent.
- Taint propagates to derivatives.
- Deletion closure is enforced or faulted.

If an invariant cannot be checked directly, the living-book version of the stack should at least record a proxy check and a residual. A weak proxy is acceptable in v0.2 when it is visible; it becomes dangerous only when the manuscript treats it as a completed proof.

## Failure modes

The failure model is part of the design, not an afterthought. This layer is expected to fail in ways that can be detected, quarantined, and used to improve the architecture.

- Memory poisoning.
- Stale or contradictory reads.
- Deleted data reappearing in derived summaries.

For Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint, mitigation starts by turning each listed failure into a named reviewable artifact. When `Memory poisoning` appears, the system should emit a residual, rejection, quarantine, regression, or human-review record rather than allowing the failure to disappear into later fluent output.

## Minimal implementation

A transaction model for context reads, writes, branches, and deletion closure.

The smallest useful implementation is deliberately narrow: A transaction model for context reads, writes, branches, and deletion closure. It should be easy to validate, easy to render in the book, and easy for a later coding run to convert into a schema, Lean predicate, deterministic fixture, trace table, or replayable command.

The implementation should be promoted only when its evidence exists. A planned test remains planned; a proof target remains planned; a prototype claim remains unpromoted until the code, command, environment, and result are recorded.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Read-your-writes snapshot test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint boundary. | planned; not run |
| Taint propagation test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint boundary. | planned; not run |
| Deletion closure test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint boundary. | planned; not run |

These tests are acceptance targets for later implementation work. They are not reported results. When a test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:vcm.transactions.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.ContextTransactions` | A snapshot read sees the committed events in its declared view. | planned |
| `lean:vcm.transactions.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.ContextTransactions` | A tainted source produces tainted derived context unless declassification is authorized. | planned |

The table records proof and contract hooks for future work. Planned hooks should become Lean proofs only when the predicate is precise enough to mechanize; otherwise they should become schemas, process contracts, or research-agenda items.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `vcm_public` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1 | memory_context | Public VCM release. Governed protocol for compiled working context. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `ladon_manhattan` | Ladon & The Manhattan Protocol | security_governance | Kernel-level security architecture for high-agency AI. | local raw cache available |
| `context_engineer` | Context Engineer / Manhattan Protocol | memory_context_lineage | Context as supply chain; compartmentalized information logistics. | local raw cache available |
| `black_hole_context_manager` | Black Hole Context Manager | memory_context_lineage | Earlier context management spec with lazy evaluation, batching, drift checks, security hardening. | local raw cache available |
| `vcm_editable` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1.0_Editable | memory_context | Editable version with evidence-carrying planner-guided context compiler framing. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The crosswalk describes why each source is assigned to the chapter and what is currently available in the repo. A local raw cache or source note makes future mining easier, but this draft still keeps the support state conservative until the specific claim-to-source mapping is recorded.

## Summary

Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint defines one boundary in the ASI Stack: VCM should use transactional memory semantics: immutable events, versioned pages, snapshots, mounts, taint, temporal validity, and deletion closure. Its job is to make a capability more governable, not merely more impressive.

In v0.2, this chapter gives Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint a complete architectural boundary: memory and context need runtime semantics for consistency, branches, contradiction, deletion, privacy, and taint. Its next maturity step is to substantiate the boundary through `Planning receives consistent views`, preserve `Snapshots are consistent`, and replace planned proof/test hooks with validated artifacts. Claims should move only when source notes, code, proofs, or run records justify the movement.
